name: Wild Bill Davison
abstract: William Edward Davison (January 5, 1906 – November 14, 1989), nicknamed "Wild Bill", was an American jazz cornetist. He emerged in the 1920s through his work playing alongside Muggsy Spanier and Frank Teschemacher in a cover band where they played the music of Louis Armstrong, but he did not achieve wider recognition until the 1940s.

quotes.text: Warming up. And, uh, at one of the concerts - one of the guys from downstairs - Carnegie Hall came and said, a lady wants to see you and she is with her husband and I said - well, who is it? They said - well, I don't know. They want to see you. So, I said - well, have them come up. We all had our own dressing rooms, at Carnegie. So, here comes a guy and his wife and it was Bix's sister and she had one of Bix's horns - the old Bach with his name on the side. And, she said -- Wild Bill, would you do me a favor - would you play this horn. This horn has never been played since my brother played it.
quotes.caption: Playing Bix Beiderbecke's horn at Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: Bill and I are the only two people alive out of that one... we made a beautiful thing together. We made two albums for Columbia... Jammin' at Condon's and East Coast West Coast Jazz.

about_quotes.speaker: Art Hodes
about_quotes.evidence: I went in there with Max Kaminsky and Freddy , and then I came back or either extended the engagement when Max had left with Wild Bill Davison and Freddy , working off as the group

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: That was with Wild Bill -- Wild Bill Davison... I got to know him backwards... we had Wild Bill Davison on trumpet... I will be in concert with him

about_quotes.speaker: Jon-Erik Kellso
about_quotes.evidence: I got to meet Wild Bill who was one of my favorites at the time and still is.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon-Erik Kellso interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Roswell Rudd
about_quotes.evidence: our main connection would be the acoustical model, the living model would have been the Eddie Condon guys in New York City. And that would be Wild Bill Davison, Pee Wee Russell, Vic Dickinson, Cutty Cutshall, all those great trombonists, and George Wettling.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roswell Rudd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: He told me that his name was Bill Davison and he was the solo man in the Chubb Steinberg orchestra that was playing at Castle Farm... He blew a couple of numbers and then we talked together during the short rest.
